one_line_takeaway: AgentCity is a detailed constitutional-governance architecture and rigorously pre-registered experimental protocol for multi-principal autonomous agent economies (agents legislate smart-contract law, software executes it, humans adjudicate via an on-chain ownership chain) — but as posted, it reports zero measured results: Section 5 states the experiments are still running and all numbers in the paper are falsification thresholds, not findings.
key_finding: There are no measured experimental results in this paper. Section 5 ('Results') states verbatim that the full experimental campaign is 'currently in progress' on an EVM-compatible L2 testnet and that 'results will be reported in a forthcoming revision.' All quantitative claims in the paper (specialization thresholds, compliance premiums, coordination-detection rates, scaling exponents, etc.) are pre-registered falsification thresholds/hypotheses, not findings — the paper is explicit about this in its own limitations list: 'All claims are pre-registered hypotheses, not measured results.' The only executed component is a n=5 mock-contract pilot confirming basic mechanic feasibility (commons-dilemma dynamics reproduce, personas are behaviorally distinguishable, and configurations are separable), not hypothesis-relevant outcomes. The paper's substantive contribution as it stands is architectural/theoretical: the SoP separation design, the three-tier contract hierarchy, the six/seven/six-stage pipelines, the formal Implementation Gap metric, the economic-security bound derivations, and the pre-registration protocol itself (including an explicit coalition-detection calibration: with 5 colluding agents submitting identical rankings among a 100-agent pool over m>=20 proposals, the resulting Kendall-tau signal is calculated to be >6 sigma from the null, versus only about 2.4 sigma at m=5 — used to argue the identical-ranking coalition case is easily detectable at scale but only marginally so for small proposal sets).
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'Separation of Power' (SoP), a constitutional governance architecture for multi-principal autonomous agent economies operating on public blockchain. SoP splits the agent 'logic chain' into three structurally isolated branches: Legislation (agents propose, deliberate, and vote to codify operational rules as smart contracts), Execution (deterministic software/contracts execute the legislated rules), and Adjudication (every agent traces to a human principal through an on-chain ownership chain, so sanctions/rewards attach to a legally accountable human). The authors instantiate SoP as AgentCity on an EVM-compatible L2 with a three-tier contract hierarchy (foundational contracts, meta-contracts, operational CollaborationContract instances), a six-stage legislative pipeline (Proposal, Committee Deliberation, Consensus Approval via Copeland/Minimax voting over full ordinal rankings, Policy Compliance Validation, Codification, Deployment Verification), a seven-stage execution pipeline (Orchestrate, Invoke, Commit, Guard, Verify, Gate, Record) with an EMA reputation update (adopted from Chen et al. 2026) and price/quality-weighted competitive bidding, and a six-stage accountability pipeline (Registration, Detection, Adjudication via an Override Panel, Sanctions, Settlement, Treasury recirculation). The core thesis is 'alignment-through-accountability': if each agent is individually accountable to its human owner, the collective converges on human-aligned behavior without top-down rule imposition. The paper also formalizes an 'Implementation Gap' inspectability metric for execution wiring topology and derives economic-security bounds (e.g., adjudicator bribery cost, Sybil/HHI-based fairness constraints on bid-pool concentration).
threat_model: Explicit and detailed, self-contained in the paper (Section 3.7 and 4.2). Five adversary classes with increasing privilege: (1) Compromised single agent (A_single) — submits fabricated outputs, inflates quality, or free-rides; bounded by the Commit-Guard-Verify stages of the execution pipeline. (2) Agent coalition (A_coalition) — colludes on legislative votes; detected via Kendall-tau correlation and Jaccard top-k overlap on full ordinal preference rankings, with detection power degrading below a constitutional coalition-size threshold (an explicitly acknowledged non-guarantee, NP-4). (3) External attacker (A_ext) — blockchain node compromise, MEV/front-running, sequencer manipulation, DoS against the off-chain execution fabric; explicitly declared out of experimental scope and delegated to the underlying L2's security model. (4) Compromised human principal (A_principal) — deliberately directs an owned agent to act against constitutional goals; the ownership chain makes this observable/sanctionable but not preventable if the principal accepts the financial loss. (5) Compromised upstream model provider (A_model) — an LLM backend adversarially conditioned at the weight level to bias outputs under trigger conditions, which can simultaneously compromise all agents sharing that backend and degrade redundant Proof-of-Progress verification (inter-executor correlation approaches rho=1); mitigated only partially via multi-provider LLM diversity. Eight named trust assumptions (TA-1..TA-8, spanning blockchain wiring integrity, on-chain non-bypassing, audit-trail completeness, cryptographic primitives, off-chain fabric integrity, model-provider honesty, dual-scorer independence, and human adjudicator good faith) gate four claimed security properties (SP-1 Wiring Integrity, SP-2 Non-Bypassing, SP-3 Full Auditability, SP-4 Separation Enforcement) and five explicit non-guarantees (NP-1 microservice internal correctness cannot be verified behaviorally, NP-2 adjudicator competence is assumed not proven, NP-3 pre-deployment malice/Trojan microservices pass identity checks but not intent checks, NP-4 sub-threshold coalitions may evade detection, NP-5 a compromised model provider degrades detection/scoring/codification simultaneously). The paper states a 'Byzantine failure ceiling': if the Adjudication branch is compromised and a majority of producer agents act adversarially, SoP provides no residual defense, and clerk agents (Registrar, Speaker, Regulator, Codifier) are treated as trusted infrastructure with clerk compromise explicitly out of scope for this version.
limitations: The paper's own Section 6 lists 14 limitations, the most consequential being: (1) all claims are pre-registered hypotheses, not measured results (no empirical evaluation exists yet); (2) real open-internet multi-principal deployment introduces adversarial dynamics the simulation does not fully capture; (3) human adjudication is simulated via a deterministic decision model rather than real legal consequences, inflating adjudicator consistency relative to production; (4) the frontier LLM backend is not yet selected, so findings (once produced) may be model-specific; (5) evaluation covers only a single economic domain (commons production); (6) Experiment 2's scaling comparison covers only the Baseline and AgentCity-Full endpoints, omitting the intermediate Emergent/Structural configurations; (7) if redundant executors share one LLM backend, Proof-of-Progress verification degrades because inter-executor correlation approaches rho=1; (8) the Byzantine failure ceiling (majority-adversarial + compromised Adjudication branch) is absolute with no architectural mitigation; (9) the treasury requires active constitutional recalibration to stay solvent (the paper itself flags that under stated steady-state defaults, monthly inflows of ~$51K fall short of ~$98.7K in disbursements); (10) the alignment thesis assumes majority-good-faith human principals and does not defend against an adversarial principal majority; (11) agents do not write raw Solidity — a Codifier agent translates approved proposals into contract code, a simplification of real agent-authored smart-contract complexity; (12) all four clerk agents are treated as trusted infrastructure with no adversarial analysis of clerk compromise; (13) the architecture does not defeat the Gibbard-Satterthwaite impossibility result for strategy-proof voting — it only raises the empirical cost of manipulation, not eliminates it; (14) agents can legislate operational contracts but cannot amend meta-contracts or the template registry, so the self-governance claim is bounded to operational scope, not institutional reform. Statistically, the paper's own power analysis flags H2h (coalition detection rate) as only exploratory-strength at its stated CDR>=0.80 threshold (55% power at 10 seeds/cell), and notes that at the smallest planned scale point (n=50) the round-100 shock pushes the adversarial fraction to about 35%, marginally exceeding the paper's own 1/3 Byzantine threshold, so results there are flagged as characterizing 'graceful degradation' rather than guaranteed safety.

baselines_compared: GovSim (Piatti et al., 2024) — ungoverned, 5 agents, single-party ownership, no enforcement
MacNet (Qian et al., 2024) — hierarchical role governance, 1,024 agents, single-party, prompt-based enforcement
Project Sid (Altera AI, 2024) — emergent norms, 1,000 agents, single-party, norm-based enforcement
Secret Collusion (Gu et al., 2024) — no governance, pairs of agents, single-party
Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) — emergent behavior, 25 agents, single-party, no enforcement
De CivAI (Dai et al., 2025) — democratic deliberation, small groups, single-party, voting-based
GEDI (Deshpande & Jin, 2024) — Condorcet voting (7 mechanisms), committees of 3-11, single-party, voting without enforcement
CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) — role-playing, agent pairs, single-party, prompt-based
Dante (Dante, 2025) — Ostrom-replication commons experiment, 5 agents, single-party, covenants plus sanctions

CMAG (CMAG Authors, 2025) — constitutional framework, small groups, single-party, multi-agent constitutional enforcement (closest architectural predecessor; differs from AgentCity in lacking multi-principal accountability, using prompt-based rather than smart-contract enforcement, and lacking a legislative branch)
